So we're on step 12, the simple basics of a great sales message. This is the last major step of the process and was formulated during a consultation with a desperate client. And it's the main template for every ad I've ever written. That client that I gave a consultation to was in a situation where everything was going to go bad because they needed a final ad, they needed it literally overnight, they had no idea how to even begin. So I sat them down and I said, look, it's pretty simple. Just explain who you are very succinctly. Here's what we've got for you, which is essentially your elevator pitch. Here's why it's important to your life right now, adding a little urgency. And here's what you need to do now, the action, the key to direct response advertising. So what that does is that answers very, very quickly what he, the prospect, needs to hear from you to decide to do what you want him to do. If you do this well, you do not need to write brilliantly. In fact, you can move quickly with shorter ads because you've covered the essentials of salesmanship if you get through this particular stuff. You can even just list your points, one, two, three, four, et cetera. I've done entire ads where I just said, I haven't got time to write an official letter to you. I just want to make sure you get each point. Number one, this is going to blah, blah, blah. Number two, this is blah, blah, blah. Number three, here's why blah, blah, blah. And you can actually do that. It doesn't look really fancy, but it's pretty straightforward and it gets the job done because it's delivering the salesmanship that is needed. This is how good salesmen are built from scratch by getting the idea of what each point is, why it needs to go into the message, where it needs to go, the emphasis put on it, all of that. So classic salesmanship secrets, these are good. There are twin targets of your pitch. And you want to give him the rational reasons to explain why he bought. As I've explained before, he needs to explain to his dubious spouse, his nosy neighbors, and his arrogant brother-in-law, and anyone else in his life. Or even himself, when he wakes up in the middle of the night, he says, why did I buy that? So you want to have the better deal. You want to have him have rational reasons at the tip of his tongue, at his fingertips, that he can remember that you told him in a memorable way why this was a better deal. Honey, I bought it because it was a better deal. Joe, the nosy neighbor, I got a really good deal on this because blah. You have to give him the ammo to be able to say that. However, at the same time, you want to appeal to the emotional eight brain reasons that seal the deal deep inside. When I say eight brain, I just mean the oldest part of our brain where everything is pretty basic. The oldest part of our brain doesn't deal with language or critical thinking or higher thought process. It deals pretty much with greed and fear. I want, I take, I'm afraid, I run away. You've probably heard of the fight or flight response. That's where this comes from. In the greed and fear, all advertising kind of breaks down to not the greed is not a bad word. It's just, it's need. It's desire. It's a strong, visceral, primitive desire for stuff. I want this. I really want it. No, I really, really, really want it. I need it more than life itself. And fear, everything from I'm afraid to miss out to I'm very afraid about what's going to happen. I need this to help me get through this. Or I'm just, I'm just generally afraid of how things are going. Sales are dropping into the tank. I need to get back to that state where I can start feeling greedy again, actually. They're not thinking this rationally. This is going on deep in their minds. This is the stuff that brings them, wakes them up in the middle of the night with a nightmare. Or just gives them that vague sense of anxiety during the day that something isn't right. So the so what test is a good one to apply here. That's an essential professional writing tactic to eliminate perfectionism, because perfectionism doesn't sell. You want to dump everything into your first draft, whether it's a script for your video, whether it's notes for your podcast, or whether it's an actual piece you're going to write that's going to go out in the mail or in a magazine or on a website. You want to dump everything into the first draft, and you want to start rewriting by editing out the stuff that doesn't pass the test. The so what test is pretty simple. If you're saying it, imagine yourself saying it to a perfect prospect sitting across from you. And if you can say so what, then it hasn't passed the test. But you're going to make claims. You're going to say this does this. And if he says, yeah, so what, then you haven't hit his sweet spot. You're not addressing his needs. You're probably dealing too much in features. You're probably not explaining the benefits enough. You're not targeted on what they want and need to hear. You're probably writing from inside yourself trying to resell yourself, or you're just way off base. So that's a very good test to put everything you write through. A lot of times when I consult with clients, I get the, but my customers are different objection. And it's just nonsense. You have to pay attention to how people actually behave, not how you wish they behaved or believe they should behave. The fundamental reality check for all marketers is that we've all got one foot still in the jungle. Denial ain't just a river in Egypt, as I wrote there. What that means is that you may think your audience is high-minded, is better educated, has more money, lives in a better part of town, and therefore their decisions are different. Somehow they have a different way of coming to a decision or deciding to buy what they're going to buy. But in truth, salesmen know it's not much different. They're satisfying the same deep desires of greed and fear, or they're answering to, they still have to answer to other people and to themselves in the middle of the night. So they need rational reasons, and they need to understand how to calm down the eight brain that is causing them to wake up in the middle of the night in a cold sweat. Everybody has the same basic emotional flowchart of what happens to them. Sometimes the problems are bigger, maybe their problems are worried about making the payroll for 100,000 people in the company, but that fear and that angst that they have is not much different than the guy who's thinking about making the payroll for the five people in his company, or the person with just one person on the payroll. You still have duties you have to meet, you have problems you have to solve, all the time big and small problems, and all of this brings us together because it's the same, we have the same brain, we're humans, we have the same brain that operates in the same way most of the time. Here's an example of a headline I wrote for a financial newsletter, which was a, the other, the competitors were all writing about very high-minded stuff, as if anybody in this market was only interested in the technicalities of getting into the stock market and being able to make more money in a rational, logical way. I decided, let's go around, let's go in the side door. So this guy I found out had a, this client that I had that wrote this financial newsletter, had been called by people, they called him the human computer because he was so good with numbers and he gets to rattle off things and do complex math equations in his head, and it was just a nickname he had, but nobody had ever called him that outside of his circle of friends and close colleagues. So by me interviewing, I found out that's kind of a hook. So the headline that went out was, Mysterious, he was mysterious a bit because he didn't, he was an introvert, he didn't hang out with, he didn't go to the seminars, he didn't hang out with a lot of the other people in his network, so they didn't know much about him. So the mysterious Arizona human computer humiliates Wall Street experts for 21st consecutive year. This is all true. For 21 years, he beat the best minds on Wall Street for returns in all kinds of different economic conditions across the board in bonds and stocks, mutual funds, he was just really, really good at what he did. But rather than come out right out and say that, this guy is really good and he beats everybody in Wall Street. I decided to bring out a heavy-duty power word, humiliate, and this creates the kind of story. So this is selling, this is moving from a very boring way to explain something that is pretty pedantic. I mean, his newsletters were pretty straightforward. It's how to invest, why you invest this way, what's coming up, things like that, not really written in an exciting way. But this made it seem that the benefit was going to be much more exciting, which it is. When you make money, you're very, very excited. Here's an example of a letter that I wrote for one of those sex education products. I think this was a video, I believe, DVDs for Rodale, one of the largest direct mailers in the world. So this was how the letter started. Dear friend, my name is Susan Smith. With your permission and with strict precautions for your privacy, I'm going to send you a fascinating package of uncensored, advanced sex class DVDs that have already helped tens of thousands of other regular people of all ages to enjoy the best sex of their lives. There's a lot of information packed in just that one split-up sentence, and I split it up for visual purposes. So Susan Smith, this is a woman writing to the prospect, which were mostly men. It was a very male-dominated market, as I recall back then. So with your permission and with strict precautions for privacy, huge when you're sending out things that have to do with sex. So I can now relax as a prospect. My kids aren't going to get it in the mail before I get to it. It's going to be safe to order this. Susan, she's going to send me a fascinating package of uncensored, advanced sex class DVDs that have already helped tens of thousands of regular people of all ages. So I don't have to feel like I'm different. There's a lot of, you know, she couldn't say tens of thousands if it wasn't true. This is a company that has a lot, has a very good reputation and a lot of trust earned. And then the kicker down here is, and guess what? For the next 21 days, you can watch and learn everything for free. So this is salesmanship come to life at the very beginning of a piece. The Professionals' Gut Check Test. This is, I think I've mentioned this before, this is mostly for copywriters who are doing freelancing. But it also is for you if you're writing for yourself. If the client likes the ad, it's probably a bomb. If you fall in love with an ad you've written, it doesn't bode well. And the reason for this is that the great salesmen who write great ads and even write for themselves, they find that point of just tolerable outrageousness or, you know, of strangeness or hook worthy storyline. And then they step over it. They just slightly go past. And I had a rule that if my client liked anything I wrote, I took it back and I would work on it some more. Because just in my 30 years, if a client likes it, it's going to bomb. Because if I haven't challenged him on his company line and what he thinks, then I've done him a disservice. Because as I've said, I want him staying up late at night, nervously sucking his thumb, wondering what's going to happen and what people are going to think of him after this ad goes out. Because that's how you get the attention of the scenario blobs out there who really aren't paying attention, who have a lot of white noise and a lot of things going on in your life that you need to break through. So here's an example of this in the golf market. Again, the client was Doc O'Leary, but this was from his father. And Mike, Doc O'Leary, told me, you know my dad, he told me the story. My dad has really pissed at me because he's getting older and he doesn't like that I'm a better golfer than him now and things like that. So I did this as what's called a lift note. Just this went out with the sales letter for DVD series. And it was a few pages long, but here's part of the first page. Hi, my name is Tom O'Leary. I'm the father of Dr. Michael O'Leary who runs the golf company OHP. Now Mike's a nice son and all that, but sometimes he just pisses me off so much I can feel my blood pressure going through the roof. You see, we golf together about twice a week. I'm 64 years old and a little worse for the wear after working hard all my life. Mike, the little SOB, is still a pipsqueak in my eyes, a young pup of only 38. So I hate it when he plays a better game of golf than I do. I've lost some juice over the years, blah, blah, blah. What that does to anybody that's paying attention, that's peer salesmanship that's coming in under the radar for anybody who can identify with Tom. Now a lot of people have identified with Dr. O'Leary who's a younger guy who knows how to talk about golf and understands. He has empathy towards older people. This, however, is coming from a guy who's walking the walk and talking the talk. So we pushed it a little bit. I got Mike to use his father as the subject of this lift note and the way he talked was, he said, he pisses me off. So we used that kind of language and it just moved everything forward. So that's a couple of good examples for the basics of a gray sales pitch. And I'll see you on the other side. Okay, simple basics of a great sales message. At this point, we're another one of those situations where most of the examples you've run through have been business to consumer. A lot of people going through the simple writing system are, they see it as I'm a business. I'm selling to not necessarily one person who will all by themselves make a decision and take out the credit card or write the check. They frequently need, okay, they got to go to someone with their boss. So you kind of talked about there's different types of the sales message. How do you write if that's the reality of your market where the person who sees your ad may have to get approval or run it past the committee or something? Well, sometimes when you're not writing to the person who can sign the check, who can make the final decision, you have to arm the person who does have it, the office manager, the low level VP, the person who has to then take it to someone who can move it up the ladder. You have to arm them to be able to carry the message. So you have to simplify things and you have to let them know. There are six reasons why this will work or why this is the software you should buy or why blah, blah, blah. Or these are the three main points you really want everyone to know when you're trying to get this. So the lower level executive may really, really want this. Let's call it software. They really want this because it makes his life easier, makes his whole department run better. So they have some insight into it that the guys in the upper offices don't know about because they're not dealing in the nuts and bolts. However, he can't go up there and say, I want the software because it makes my life easier because they'll say, no, we don't care. We hire you to suffer. So you can know that. So by knowing that, that's an advantage. So then you can say, look, at the end of the day, this is going to make your life easier. It's going to make your department look a lot better. And if there's any downsizing going on, you're going to be the last place they're going to look to cut people's jobs. Some along that line. However, when you're explaining it to them, you know, here is the way that they need to think about it. Overall costs are going to go down. They're going to see better results very, very quickly. It's easy. They don't need to hire anybody to take care of it, to install it. They're not getting in bed with any great degree of risk with a company where they have to use proprietary things or whatever. So that then he has very succinct points. He can actually make a note card and go in and say, boss, we really need the software. Here's why. And then you've given them the reasons and he maps it out. Does that make sense? Yeah, I think it does. I've even seen that commonly used in the event industry where you're trying to get the attendees will be employees of large corporations traditionally. They are quite comfortable putting right in their marketing on the website or in the mail that you might get as the person. Bring this, in other words, here's the pitch. Now bring this one page to your boss or copy paste this into your request for approval. So that is a proven way to market things that don't count on the person knowing how to summarize it up. And like we said, now that you've introduced and what to do now, that is maybe part of what to do now. Tell your boss these exact points. Exactly. And also trusting someone who's even passionate about it, no matter how passionate that person is about the product. They're going to screw it up if you don't give them tips of what to do. They'll go in and they have their own idea of how to sell it to the boss because the boss is a very scary man and you have to approach him. And it's just all kinds of reasons why they'll screw it up. They're not trained salesmen within the corporation. They're probably sub level executives for a reason. And so you are on me more than that. I think you said with that, they have not been through the simple writing system. They're not going to have your discipline. Okay. Let's say you're working on an ad, whether it's a video or going to the mail, go and email, and you believe the recipient knows who you are. Do you still take the time to introduce yourself or do you just hit the ground running? No, you do. No matter how hot you are, no matter what a great person you are, your prospects and your customers, even your longtime customers, you aren't in the forefront of their mind all the time. They have trouble remembering their kids' names. They have a lot going on from the last time. If you're not contacting them five or six times a day more than their regular family is, then you have to be reminded of, wait a minute, who are you again? And it just takes a long time. So develop shortcut little ways of saying, here's who I am. That's why I talk about doing this succinctly. If you remember, I'm the guy that's been sending you emails all week. They've been arriving in your inbox. Quite regularly, we're talking about the kind of life change with software that will make your life easier and your boss happier and may even get you a promotion. So let's get down to business. Here's what's going on today and you move on from there. So yes, always remind people who you are. But don't take a long time doing it. Learn how to do it succinctly, which means very short, Tithy. Okay. Last common question from this section. You've mentioned Doc O'Leary, who actually is a real person, but you're writing, you kind of amplified his voice, but he was in the back of your mind. An example of a guy who was okay, at least he was going to be the hero in the ads. As much as we emphasize that to a lot of people in small businesses, we've been down the road before. So they don't want to do that. There's just people out there saying, look, it's either it is, it's important or I believe it's important or I'm shy. Can I make up a name? Is it like, can I just make up a pretend character and go by this name? So pen name, nom de plume. How does that play in the real world? Yes, absolutely. It's very, very acceptable. I could have, I actually gave Doc O'Leary the idea, can you be Doc O'Malley? And he said, I don't care. Just use my name. And so I would rather do that. That to me is best because then there's no, it's just very strange. It's just very straightforward and very honest. However, some people don't want their name out there. I totally understand that there's huge privacy concerns and things. So it's okay to use a pen name. Famous authors use it, do it all the time. And it's a accepted part of business culture that as long as you're paying your taxes and you can be identified, so you're not hiding from your responsibilities, but in business it's okay to present a different person. I've written in so many different people's personalities, for example, one of the examples, and this was Susan Smith, for a very large corporation. That is actually a made up name. I think they actually got someone to sign it and to agree to kind of be the person who would be that, but she didn't want her name used, so we changed the name a little bit. And that's a major company, and I'm writing in the voice of a woman. So it kind of startles people when they find out a lot of the romance novels in the world are written by men. They're written by women, they have women's names, and they're written to women, and yet they're actually, the actual author is a man whose job is to write like a woman to a woman's audience. It goes the other way too. There are women that have written very well to men's audiences, and the men don't know they're actually dealing with a female. We do that all the time. Yes. Women, we work with. Okay, I think that wraps up this section. This is an exciting exercise. It's all starting to come together in a big way. Some new things, and some tightening up, some things we've already worked on. John will explain the details right after this. So here's the exercise for step 12. I want you to just fill out the four little items here. Now, we've gone through some of this before in the simple writing system, but this is kind of putting it together in a nice little tight package that you should be able to easily get through. So right as if you're speaking in the elevator or in the bar, you're turning to someone and you're just explaining it. Here's who I am, succinctly. Here's what I've got for you, which is basically your elevator pitch. Keep it very, very short. One, two sentences. Here's why it's important for your life right now. Keep it very, very short. One or two sentences. Here's what to do now. So it's here. Here's who I am. My name is John Carlton. What I have for you is a shortcut series of steps that will help you write any kind of sales message you want whenever you need it. It's important for you if you're in business because until you get these things written, your business will flounder. There's no way that you can sell a lot without having some kind of sales message. Here's what to do now. Go to this link, click on it, and we'll take you through the process of signing up for this. So that would be an example. So now I want you to do it, and good luck.
